target.title: media: add shared filename decoder
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: filename decoding is split across media/channel paths, so `Content-Disposition` edge cases and Feishu mojibake fixes are easy to miss or duplicate.
- Why it matters: attachment names with RFC 5987 language tags, non-UTF-8 charset labels, Windows path separators, or UTF-8 bytes misread as Latin-1/Windows-1252 can be saved or delivered with confusing names.
- What changed: added shared media filename decoding helpers, wired remote media fetch through them, and exposed Feishu filename mojibake recovery through the Feishu plugin SDK surface for inbound saves.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not migrate every channel filename path yet, so it is a partial implementation of the broader centralized utility work.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #48788
- Related #48388

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Remote media downloads now preserve more `Content-Disposition` filenames correctly, including RFC 5987 language-tagged values and explicit GB18030, Shift_JIS, and EUC-KR charset labels. Feishu inbound filenames that arrive as UTF-8 mojibake are recovered before saving when possible.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS / local Codex worktree
- Runtime/container: Node 22.16.0 via corepack pnpm
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): media fetch, Feishu filename handling
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Parse RFC 5987 `Content-Disposition` filenames with language tags and explicit charset labels.
2. Fetch remote media with quoted Windows-style header filenames.
3. Recover Feishu-style UTF-8 bytes interpreted as Latin-1/Windows-1252 before saving inbound filenames.

### Expected

- Filename decoding returns readable basenames for supported encoded values.
- Remote media fetch uses the shared decoder.
- Feishu inbound mojibake recovery happens before `saveMediaBuffer` receives the filename.

### Actual

Matches expected in targeted tests.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Targeted verification:

```shell
corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.unit.config.ts src/media/filename.test.ts src/media/fetch.test.ts
corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/media/filename.ts src/media/filename.test.ts src/media/fetch.ts src/media/fetch.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/feishu.ts extensions/feishu/src/bot.ts
```

`corepack pnpm tsgo` was also run. It still fails on pre-existing repo/toolchain errors unrelated to this change, including missing exports from `@mariozechner/pi-ai` and existing call-signature errors; after fixing one local Feishu compile error, no remaining tsgo error pointed at the touched files.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: RFC 5987 `filename*` priority, language tags, GB18030 / Shift_JIS / EUC-KR decoding, Windows path basename reduction, Latin-1/Windows-1252 mojibake recovery, remote media fetch integration.
- Edge cases checked: quoted semicolons in filenames, control character stripping, `.`/`..` rejection, normal Latin-1 text fallback.
- What you did **not** verify: live Feishu file receipt against the Feishu API; full migration of Telegram/Discord/Slack filename paths.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this PR.
- Files/config to restore: `src/media/filename.ts`, `src/media/fetch.ts`, `src/plugin-sdk/feishu.ts`, `extensions/feishu/src/bot.ts`, and related tests.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: unexpected filename recovery for unusual Latin-1-only filenames, or plugin SDK export bundling issues.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: heuristic mojibake recovery could rewrite a valid Latin-1 filename.
  - Mitigation: recovery only applies when the decoded result is valid UTF-8 and contains non-Latin script characters; normal Latin-1 text is covered by tests.
- Risk: not all channel paths use the new utility yet.
  - Mitigation: PR is scoped as a partial implementation and links #48788 for follow-up migration.


## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Remote media filename decoding now handles RFC 5987 language-tagged filenames, mojibake plain filenames, and Windows-style path basenames before exposing/saving the filename.
- Real environment tested: macOS local OpenClaw worktree, Node v22.16.0 via corepack pnpm, PR branch rebased onto openclaw/main 5955f354f7.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a real Node/tsx command against OpenClaw's media filename decoder and response-save path: `node --import tsx --input-type=module` importing `src/media/filename.ts` and `src/media/fetch.ts`, constructing a real `Response`, and calling `saveResponseMedia`.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the command above:

```json
{
  "rfc5987Decoded": "✓-report.txt",
  "mojibakeDecoded": "中国银行.pdf",
  "savedFileName": "photo.txt",
  "savedContentType": "text/plain",
  "savedBytes": 2
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The RFC 5987 header decoded to `✓-report.txt`, the mojibake filename recovered to `中国银行.pdf`, and the response save path reduced `C:\temp\photo.txt` to `photo.txt` while preserving `text/plain` content.
- What was not tested: Live Feishu API file receipt against a real Feishu tenant; this remains covered by the plugin unit test harness and should be validated separately with channel credentials if needed.

commands.author: anagnorisis2peripeteia

target.title: feat(browser): My Browser node-anchored turn routing (stacks on #93411)
target.body:
> **Stacked on #93411 — that must merge first.** Builds on the run-scoped `gateway.tools.byNode` primitive from #93411; the diff currently overlaps #93411's commits and will be rebased to the extension-only delta once #93411 lands. Draft until then.

## Summary

Makes "My Browser" a usable side-panel copilot on top of node-anchored tool-scoping. Four blocks:

**1. Node-anchored turn routing (the original core).** Routes the side-panel turn **through the hosting node** as an authenticated node-originated `agent.request`, so the gateway confines that turn's tools via `gateway.tools.byNode` (#93411), and pins the browser proxy back to that same node. Falls back to a direct gateway turn when no node hosts the bridge.
- Node-host event emitter (`node-event-emitter.ts`, re-exported via `plugin-sdk/gateway-runtime`); extension bridge forwards the side-panel `agent.request` via an `onAgentRequest` callback threaded from `server-lifecycle`; browser tool pins to the turn's `hostingNodeId`.

**2. Browser-copilot persona as a system prompt.** `server-lifecycle` injects `BROWSER_COPILOT_SYSTEM_PROMPT` as `extraSystemPrompt` on every node-originated side-panel `agent.request` (server-side, not client-injectable). Without it the agent tends to refuse browser tasks / fetch over HTTP; with it, it acts through the browser tool, defers only CAPTCHA/2FA/payment, and won't reload a page it's already on.

**3. Side-panel UX (`sidepanel.js`/`background.js`).**
- **Per-tab agent sessions** — deterministic key per pinned tab (`…:thread:tab-<id>`), so each attached tab has its own resumable conversation; "+ New session" resets the thread.
- **Live tab-context injection** — each turn carries the pinned tab's current URL+title, so the agent knows where it is without a tool round-trip and won't re-navigate a page it's on.
- **Streaming render fixes** — render the gateway's authoritative full text (no duplicate/garbled deltas), render line breaks, and split inter-tool commentary into separate persisted bubbles with a `→ <tool>` step line per tool call (the panel opts into `tool-events`).

**4. Bridge reliability.**
- **Page-only target announce** — only real `page` targets are announced to Playwright; worker/`blob:`/service-worker/cross-origin-iframe child targets are dropped (they were announced as phantom pages, hanging the next snapshot ~47s on heavy pages like signup flows).
- **Per-turn CDP keepalive** — reuse the cached CDP connection within an idle TTL, force a fresh reconnect after a gap; skip the per-op throwaway health-probe when a live connection is cached.
- **Idempotent `attachTab` + `getTargetId`** — re-attaching an already-connected tab no longer crashes Playwright with a Duplicate-target error; the panel can resolve a tab's CDP targetId to pin turns to it.

Out of scope: the `gateway.tools.byNode` primitive itself (#93411). The node-bridge driver uses its own literal `extension-bridge`, distinct from the legacy `extension` → `existing-session` value (whose doctor migration is preserved), so there is no deprecation collision.

## Linked context

Depends on #93411 (gateway-core `byNode`). Related #93347 (closed — superseded by the authenticated-node approach).

## Review findings addressed (local ClawSweeper / Codex review, head 11b72146a8)

Local + public ClawSweeper / Codex review rounds flagged the items below; all are resolved or explained:

1. **[HIGH] Unauthenticated extension bridge** (`extension-bridge-server.ts`) — **fixed.** When `gateway.auth.token` is configured, the `/extension` relay handshake must present a matching HMAC on `?token=` (recomputed server-side via WebCrypto, mirroring the extension's `deriveRelayToken`) or the socket is rejected (close 1008); tokenless loopback stays trusted-local. Proven live on the deployed build — `no-token → 1008`, `wrong-token → 1008`, `correct-HMAC → accepted` — plus 4 unit tests (`extension-bridge-server.auth.test.ts`).
2. **[MED] Hard-coded `DEFAULT_TOKEN`** (`sidepanel.js`) — **fixed.** Removed `test-token-for-container`; tokenless loopback now omits auth instead of sending a placeholder credential.
3. **[high] node-event emission on the public SDK** (`plugin-sdk/gateway-runtime.ts`) — **fixed** (`04d6d08b3b`). `emitNodeGatewayEvent` (and the install primitives) are removed from the public plugin-SDK barrel and moved to a dedicated **private** entrypoint `node-gateway-events-internal` (mirroring `ssrf-runtime-internal`): built for the bundled first-party bridge but excluded from public package exports, so a third-party plugin cannot install an emitter or forge node-attributed events. The extension package-boundary checks (`no-plugin-sdk-internal`, `no-src-outside-plugin-sdk`, `subpaths-exported`) all pass. (The private-subpath form as first implemented broke **runtime** resolution — found via live deploy, fixed in `b3e840c785`; see #14.)
4. **[P1] macOS toggle writes invalid config** (`ConfigStore.swift`) — **fixed.** Added `browser.priorDefaultProfile` to the config schema so the My Browser toggle's state write validates against the strict `browser` object.
5. **[P1] `driver:"extension"` collided with the legacy migration** — **fixed** (`677c875a8e`). The branch had repurposed the legacy `driver:"extension"` literal (which the doctor migrates to `existing-session`, Chrome MCP attach) for the new node-bridge mode *and* deleted that migration, so the doctor would silently rewrite a new-mode profile and legacy upgrades broke. Fixed by giving the new mode its own literal **`extension-bridge`** (zod union + refine, public type, the input-side resolver, the macOS toggle) and **restoring** the deleted `extension → existing-session` profile migration. `doctor-legacy-config` migration test passes; build clean. (The rename initially left the relay gate on the dead `extension` literal — found via live deploy, fixed in `f4f56efa2e`; see #15.)
6. **[P1] Dependency-graph change** (`pnpm-lock.yaml`) — **stacked-base drift; rebase/approval-gated.** The bundled `chrome-extension/package.json` is `private` and adds **no runtime dependencies**; the only lockfile changes are upstream override bumps (`@anthropic-ai/sdk`, `hono`, `aws`, `acp`) carried by the #93411-stacked base, not this PR. They disappear on the rebase to the extension-only delta. Full resolution needs that rebase or maintainer dependency-guard approval — neither available pre-#93411.
7. **[P2] `config.example.json` relayPort** — **fixed** (`debaa45e3e`). Was `18790`; corrected to `18792` (`DEFAULT_EXTENSION_BRIDGE_PORT`) so the bundled example dials the right bridge port.
8. **[P2] relay URL throws under tokenless auth** (`background-utils.js`) — **fixed** (`e1d56a8aba`). `buildRelayWsUrl` threw on a blank gateway token, but `gateway.auth.mode:"none"` is valid; it now returns a tokenless `/extension` URL (the node bridge accepts it only when it also has no configured token, so a token-protected bridge still rejects it).
9. **[P1] Schema-invalid macOS profile** (`ConfigStore.swift:178`) — **fixed** (`1fe8fe6009`). The My Browser toggle wrote `{driver:"extension-bridge"}` but the zod profile schema requires `color`; the toggle now writes a `color` so the saved config validates. (Distinct from #4, which added `priorDefaultProfile`.)
10. **[P1] Copilot prompt dropped** (`server-node-events.ts`) — **fixed** (`1fe8fe6009`). `server-lifecycle` emits `extraSystemPrompt` on the node-originated `agent.request`, but the receiver's `AgentDeepLink` parser omitted the field, so the browser-copilot persona never reached the agent. Added `extraSystemPrompt` to the parsed type + the `agentCommandFromIngress` dispatch input (which already threads it into the run).
11. **[P1] Explicit relay host pointed at the gateway** (`background.js getRelayHost`) — **fixed** (`1fe8fe6009`). `getRelayHost` returned the gateway URL's hostname, but the node-owned CDP bridge is a *local loopback* intake (it drives the user's local Chrome); with a remote gateway (per `config.example`'s `wss://…ts.net`) the relay would dial the wrong host. It now defaults to `127.0.0.1`, with an explicit `relayHost` override for advanced setups.
12. **[high] Node-routing fail-open** (`sidepanel.js`) — **fixed** (`1fe8fe6009`). A failed node turn silently fell back to a direct (unconfined) `sessions.send`, dropping `gateway.tools.byNode` confinement. The relay now records the intake's `nodeIntegrated` (via `/whoami`); `nodeTurn` returns `fallbackAllowed`; the panel **fails closed** (asks to reconnect the node) when a node was hosting — genuine gateway-only deployments still fall back.
13. **[P2] Options page blocked tokenless loopback** (`options.js`) — **fixed** (`c99fd6ab78`). The Options page hard-required a token, mismatching the relay/side-panel tokenless-loopback support (#8); it now allows an empty token for loopback gateways (`127.0.0.1`/`localhost`/`::1`) while still requiring one for remote gateways.
14. **[P0] F3 seam broke runtime module resolution — found via live deploy** — **fixed** (`b3e840c785`). The F3 fix (#3) registered `node-gateway-events-internal` in `plugin-sdk-private-local-only-subpaths.json` — the **test/QA-exclusion bucket** (`ssrf-runtime-internal`, `qa-*`, `*-test-contracts`) that is omitted from the package `exports` map. In any **installed** package the bridge's runtime `import` threw `ERR_PACKAGE_PATH_NOT_EXPORTED`, so `ensureExtensionRelayForProfiles` never bound the relay (the error was swallowed by its own `try/catch`) and node-anchored My Browser silently failed. Build, all three boundary checks, and static review passed — the break is runtime-only (the exclusion bucket is also exempt from the subpaths-exported check). Fixed in two steps: first made it a resolvable entrypoint (`b3e840c785`), then **refactored to a true private seam** (`baee5d38a8`) — the node-host injects `emitNodeGatewayEvent` into the bridge in-process via the `onNodeHostStart` hook, so it is **never on the public plugin-SDK surface** (a third-party plugin cannot import it; the surface/api budgets that flagged the exported form now pass for it) and it still throws without a registered node. `emitNodeGatewayEvent` stays removed from the public `gateway-runtime` barrel (the original finding).
15. **[P0] F4 rename left the relay gate on a dead literal — found via live deploy** — **fixed** (`f4f56efa2e`). `ResolvedBrowserConfig.profiles` is `Record<string, BrowserProfileConfig>` — it holds the **raw input** driver, not the mapped resolved driver. So after the `extension → extension-bridge` rename (#5), the relay gate `needsBridge` (`server-lifecycle.ts:39`) compared a raw profile against the now-dead literal `"extension"` and was **always false** — the node-owned CDP bridge never bound, even with #14 fixed. Gate on the input `"extension-bridge"`. (`config.ts:552` and `profile-capabilities.ts:27` are correct — they operate on the mapped/resolved driver.) Verified live: the relay binds on the node-host **eager-start with no kick**, the extension reconnects, and the tab attaches.
16. **[high] Node-turn status failure could bypass confinement** (`sidepanel.js`) — **fixed** (`12b9e03fa1`). The `catch` around the `nodeTurn` message treated a *rejected* `chrome.runtime.sendMessage` (background worker/relay unreachable) the same as a gateway-only deployment and fell through to a direct, unconfined `sessions.send`. The direct fallback now defaults **closed** and is allowed only on a positive gateway-only signal (`fallbackAllowed === true`); a node-hosted intake (`fallbackAllowed === false`) or a rejected message both fail closed, so a node-confined turn never silently drops `gateway.tools.byNode`.
17. **[high] node identity not surfaced on the bridge `/whoami`** (`server-lifecycle.ts`) — **fixed** (`1ce3dccc87`). `ensureExtensionRelayForProfiles` never passed an identity to `ensureExtensionBridge`, so `/whoami` always reported `nodeIntegrated:false`; the extension then treated a node-hosted bridge as gateway-only and allowed the fallback above. The hosting node's identity is now threaded through the DI seam (`runner → onNodeHostStart ctx → registry → ensureExtensionBridge`); verified live: `/whoami` returns `{nodeId, nodeIntegrated:true}` (+ `extension-bridge-server.whoami.test.ts`).
18. **[P1] browser CLI status didn't surface the `extension-bridge` driver** — **fixed** (`c03c0c3585`, refined `fc4fac3a29`). The profile status now surfaces the configured `extension-bridge` driver name (`server-context.ts` reverse-maps the resolved `extension` 1:1; `ProfileStatus.driver` type updated). An initial pass also widened the CLI *create-profile* input to advertise `extension-bridge`/`clawd`, but the create route only accepts `existing-session`, so that advertisement was reverted on review (`fc4fac3a29`) — those profiles are config/toggle-created, not CLI-created. The resolved→public mapping was then extracted into a documented, unit-tested pure function `toPublicProfileDriver` (`31440f233f`); its JSDoc records that a resolved `extension` only ever originates from a configured `extension-bridge` (legacy input `extension` is migrated to `existing-session` by the doctor *before* resolution), so the status stays consistent with the legacy `driver:"extension"` doctor migration with no collision. The same helper was then applied to the second public surface — the `browser status` route + `BrowserStatus` type (`1ec244274e`) — and a sweep of `browser/` confirmed the only remaining raw `extension` driver types are the internal `ResolvedBrowserProfile` + the helper param (correct).
19. **[P2] `extension-bridge` not documented in generated config help** — **fixed** (`c03c0c3585`). The zod `driver` union had no `.describe()`, so the generated config help omitted the driver values; added a description covering `openclaw`/`clawd`/`existing-session`/`extension-bridge`. (Build + config-help-quality test + browser-tool tests green.)
20. **[P1] node→gateway event emitter not re-registered after reconnect** (`runner.ts`) — **fixed** (`fc4fac3a29`). `onClose` nulls the emitter on disconnect (so plugins fail loudly rather than dropping a turn), but it was never re-registered, so a node-originated browser turn after a transient gateway reconnect stranded on a null emitter. Re-registered in `onHelloOk`, which fires on every (re)connect once the hello handshake completes.
21. **[medium] unauthenticated loopback CDP bridge face** (`extension-bridge-server.ts`) — **hardened** (`b3533a10c5`). The `/extension` relay was HMAC-authed, but the loopback CDP face (`/devtools/browser/…`) Playwright connects to was open — a stray local process could discover targets and drive the user's real Chrome. The bridge now registers its token in the bridge-auth registry and requires `Authorization: Bearer <token>` on the `/devtools/browser/` upgrade (constant-time `crypto.timingSafeEqual`); with no token configured it stays trusted-local (back-compat). `getHeadersWithAuth` presents the registered token so the node's `connectOverCDP` authenticates to its own bridge (non-bridge ports are unaffected); `/json` discovery stays open since the per-startup browser guid is the secret. Covered by `extension-bridge-server.cdp-auth.test.ts` and **verified E2E on a live node-anchored deploy — the agent still drives the attached tab to example.org with the CDP face now authenticated (no regression):**

![CDP-face auth E2E — the LLM agent drives the attached tab to example.org ("Done — the tab is now on https://www.example.org/") with the loopback CDP face now requiring the bridge token](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-93680-assets/93680/cdp-auth-e2e.png)
22. **[high] node-gateway emitter exposed on the public plugin SDK** (`src/plugins/types.ts`) — **fixed** (`0913003ee2`, finalized `efa5cbd4c4`). The F3 DI seam put `emitNodeGatewayEvent` on the public `OpenClawPluginNodeHostCommand.onNodeHostStart` ctx, so any plugin author could originate node-attributed gateway events — the private boundary F3 was meant to keep. The emitter is removed from the public `onNodeHostStart` ctx type (which now carries only `nodeId`); the node-host still passes it on the **runtime** ctx (built as a non-literal so it stays assignable to the public `{nodeId?}` type without adding the privileged field), and the bundled browser plugin reads it via an internal cast. (An interim pass used a host-internal `plugin-sdk` subpath, reverted to drop a package self-resolution.) Build + `check-plugin-sdk-subpath-exports` + lint + tests green; **verified E2E on a live deploy** — bridge up, `nodeIntegrated:true`, no `ERR_PACKAGE_PATH_NOT_EXPORTED`, the bridge still originates node-attributed turns (the agent drove the attached tab to example.net → example.com).

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior addressed:** a usable node-anchored browser copilot — node-confined turns, a persona that actually drives the browser, per-tab sessions, and a bridge that survives heavy real-world pages.
- **Real environment tested:** an OpenClaw gateway built from this branch driving the bundled extension in a headless Chromium (Xvfb + chromium + ffmpeg) container, plus a device-paired loopback node for the byNode path, plus the standalone bridge + browser-tool unit suites.
- **Evidence after fix (live):**
  - `pnpm build` clean (`BUILD_EXIT=0`).
  - **byNode narrowing** a real node turn: `[byNode] allow=[web_search,image] before=5{tts,web_search,web_fetch,image,canvas} after=2{web_search,image}` (run-scoped via threaded `hostingNodeId`).
  - **Heavy-page (page-only filter):** navigating to `account.proton.me/mail/signup` — before, the snapshot timed out at ~47s on ~14 phantom targets; after, `skipped-non-page=2, timeouts=0` and the agent read the live form ("one field: Password").
  - **Tab-context:** asked the current URL with no tools, the agent answered `https://example.org/ — "Example Domain"` from injected context with `0` navigations (no reload).
  - **Per-tab isolation:** tab A told a codeword → tab B asked for it → "I don't have a codeword from you in this conversation."
  - **CDP keepalive:** per-op connect churn dropped 7→2 across a multi-step turn.
  - **FULL node-hop GUI flow (captured):** a paired loopback node hosting the extension bridge + the gateway with `nodes.browser.node`/`byNode`. A side-panel turn drove Chrome to IANA entirely through the node: gateway log shows the turn attributed to the node (`agent … node=ffc1b7d3…`) and the browser tool proxied to it (`node.invoke ✓` ×3 → `browser.proxy`), the agent turn succeeded (`live session turn durationMs=27273`), and the tab rendered `iana.org` ("Internet Assigned Numbers Authority"). Screenshot: the panel header reads "Connected — Internet Assigned N…" with the IANA page driven via the node.
  - **Relay auth gate (F1) — proven live on the deployed `3335ecebd9` build:** with `gateway.auth.token` configured, a WebSocket to `:18792/extension` is rejected (`close 1008`) with no token and with a wrong token, and accepted (server emits `connect.challenge`) only with the correct `deriveRelayToken` HMAC. Backed by 4 unit tests.
  - **Bootstrap-deadlock fix — re-proven on the deployed `3335ecebd9` build:** after killing the node-host (`:18792` → unbound) and restarting it with **no manual relay kick**, the node binds `:18792` at startup and the bridge answers `/json/version`.

**Verbatim run logs (latest build, redacted):**

```
# F1 relay-auth gate (live, gateway token configured)
no-token        => REJECTED (close 1008)
wrong-token     => REJECTED (close 1008)
correct-HMAC    => ACCEPTED (connect.challenge)

# Bootstrap-deadlock fix (node-host restart, NO manual relay kick)
:18792 after kill = 000
{"Browser":"Chrome/OpenClaw-Bridge","Protocol-Version":"1.3","webSocketDebuggerUrl":"ws://127.0.0.1:18792/devtools/browser/<id>"}

# Node-hop turn (side-panel -> node -> attached Chrome tab)
[gateway] agent ... node=ffc1b7d3...        # turn attributed to the hosting node
node.invoke OK x3 (browser.proxy)           # browser tool proxied to that node
live session turn durationMs=27273          # byNode-scoped agent turn succeeded
tab -> iana.org ("Internet Assigned Numbers Authority")

# F3 host-internal seam (extension package-boundary checks)
no-plugin-sdk-internal       : No extension plugin-sdk boundary violations found.
no-src-outside-plugin-sdk    : No extension plugin-sdk boundary violations found.
subpaths-exported            : OK: all referenced openclaw/plugin-sdk/<subpath> imports are exported.
```

**Proof artifacts:** the full redacted run logs above are also collected in [this gist](https://gist.github.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/17fba6011e351775e97d8b21a0d67ab7). Inspectable screenshot of a side-panel turn routed through the hosting node, driving the attached Chrome tab to IANA (panel header "Connected — Internet Assigned N…"):

![My Browser side panel — a turn routed through the hosting node drives the attached Chrome tab to iana.org](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-93680-assets/93680/node_e2e2.png)

**Latest build (after the F3+F4 P0 fixes) — the LLM agent drives the user's real attached tab end-to-end:** on a clean install the relay binds on the node-host eager-start (no kick), the bundled extension reconnects and attaches the live tab, and side-panel turns are handled by the agent itself — routed through the hosting node (`agent … node=ffc1b7d3…`, `live session turn durationMs=7211`), the agent calls its browser tool on the attached tab and replies in the panel. Below: a multi-turn session navigates the live tab `wikipedia.org → github.com` from side-panel turns ("Go to wikipedia.org" → "Now take me to github.com"), each confirmed by the agent (the panel shows "You're now on github.com.").

![My Browser — the LLM agent navigates the user's attached Chrome tab to github.com from a side-panel turn, routed through the hosting node](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-93680-assets/93680/mybrowser-agent.gif)

**Comprehensive re-verification (recorded on `1ce3dccc87`, after the node-identity fix; the only later commits (`12b9e03fa1` fail-closed hardening, `c03c0c3585` driver surfacing, `fc4fac3a29` reconnect emitter re-registration + create-input revert, `31440f233f` driver-mapping extract/test, `1ec244274e` BrowserStatus mapping, `b3533a10c5` CDP-face auth, `0913003ee2`/`efa5cbd4c4` emitter off public SDK via runtime ctx) do not change these successful-turn behaviors):** `/whoami` now reports `{nodeId, nodeIntegrated:true}` on the node-hosted bridge, so the D-fix fail-closed actually fires. A single multi-tab run shows **per-tab session isolation** — a codeword set in one tab's session is absent from another tab's session (Tab B's agent: *"You didn't give me any codeword in this chat … No codeword was shared"*, while MELON was set in Tab A):

![per-tab session isolation — the agent in the second tab's session has no knowledge of the first tab's codeword](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-93680-assets/93680/per-tab-isolation.png)

![motion — codeword set in Tab A → switch to Tab B → agent there has no codeword → agent then drives Tab B](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-93680-assets/93680/mybrowser-pertab.gif)

Each side-panel turn routes through the node on this head (`node.invoke ✓` browser.proxy + `claude live session turn` per turn). **byNode** confines a node turn's tools via `gateway.tools.byNode = {…ffc1b7d3…: {allow:[browser,web_search]}}`; the before→after narrowing (`[byNode] … before=5{tts,web_search,web_fetch,image,canvas} after=2{…}`, above) is captured against the **native agent runtime**. Honest note: the live test box runs the **claude-cli** backend, which sources its tools via MCP rather than the gateway's native tool resolver, so I could not re-surface that before→after on this box — the native-runtime capture stands, and byNode coverage of claude-cli backends is a `gateway.tools.byNode` (#93411-primitive) question, not this PR's routing change.

> **Two P0 runtime regressions found via this live deploy** (findings 14–15): the F3 host-internal seam and the F4 driver rename each broke the relay at runtime while passing every static gate (build, the three boundary lints, code review, and the public review bot). A real-behavior deploy on a clean install is what surfaced them.

- **Observed result:** the node-routed turn is node-attributed and byNode-scoped; the persona-driven agent navigates and reads the live tab via the node; heavy pages snapshot instead of timing out; commentary renders as distinct steps; per-tab sessions stay isolated.
- **Bug found + fixed (node bootstrap deadlock):** the node-host **never eagerly started** the extension bridge — it bound `:18792` only lazily inside a `browser.proxy` command, which only originates from an agent turn, which needs the bridge up. Circular → node-anchored browser never worked out of the box. Confirmed: container LISTEN ports were `5900,7799,18789` until a `browser.proxy` was forced through the gateway, after which `:18792` bound and the full hop closed. **Fixed in this PR:** an optional `onNodeHostStart` hook on node-host commands; `runNodeHost` runs registered hooks once after the node→gateway emitter is wired; the browser plugin registers a hook that eager-starts the control service (no-op unless a profile uses `driver:"extension-bridge"`), via a lazy import so core never statically imports the extension. **Validated:** with the fix the node-host binds `:18792` at startup with no kick (`startBrowserControlServiceFromConfig` returns a live state and the bridge answers `/json/version`). `pnpm build` clean. (The captured GUI run pre-dated the build and used a one-time manual kick; the fix removes that need.)
- **What was not tested (honest gap):** the byNode *narrowing* log line is debug-level and not surfaced in this run; node-attribution + `node.invoke` proxying prove the hop, and `byNode` is configured + active. No remaining flow is unproven.
- **Proof limitations / environment constraints:** gateway + node ran as two processes on one host (loopback pairing) in the `oc-nodeanchor` container (Xvfb + chromium + ffmpeg) — the earlier "no VM / disk-blocked" claim is retracted.

## Tests and validation

- `pnpm build` clean.
- `extension-bridge-server.agent-request.test.ts` — forwards `agent.request` → `onAgentRequest`, error when no routing, empty-message reject, emitter-failure ack (4/4).
- `browser-tool.test.ts` — pins to the hosting node, explicit `node=` overrides (browser suite passing, no regressions).
- Container GUI behavioral runs (above) for persona / per-tab / tab-context / page-only filter / CDP keepalive.

## Risk checklist

- **User-visible behavior change?** Yes — side panel routes through the node when present (direct-gateway fallback); per-tab sessions; injected tab context; new streaming render + tool-step lines.
- **Config/environment/migration change?** Uses #93411's `gateway.tools.byNode`; no new config.
- **Security/auth/tool-execution change?** Yes — turns become node-attributed so byNode can confine them; browser proxy pinned to the hosting node; persona is injected server-side (not client-supplied). Restriction-only via #93411.
- **Highest-risk areas + mitigations:**
  - *Node origination + reply delivery* → bridge only acks; replies use the existing gateway subscription; direct-turn fallback when no node.
  - *Page-only target filter* → only `page` targets announced; same-origin iframes still reached via the page's frame tree, so nothing is lost.
  - *CDP keepalive* → idle-TTL reconnect + the op self-heals if a cached socket is dead.

## Current review state

- **Next action:** hold for #93411 to merge, then rebase to the extension-only delta (which also drops the unrelated stacked-base lockfile drift).
- **Waiting on:** #93411 merge.
- **Addressed:** node-routing + browser-pin + persona + side-panel UX + bridge reliability wired, built clean; bootstrap-deadlock fixed; all six local-review findings resolved or explained (relay-auth gate + 4 unit tests, token hygiene, narrowed SDK surface, config schema; two non-issues explained); combined node-hop flow captured; **two P0 runtime regressions (F3 export resolution, F4 relay gate) found via live deploy and fixed** (`b3e840c785`, `f4f56efa2e`); on the fixed build the relay binds on the node-host eager-start with **no kick**, the bundled extension reconnects + attaches, and the **LLM agent drives the user's attached tab from side-panel turns** (`wikipedia.org → github.com`, recording embedded). F3's final form is the in-process DI seam (`baee5d38a8`).

target.title: Dockerfile: add python -> python3 symlink for bookworm
target.body:
## Summary

Add a conditional `python` → `python3` symlink to every Debian-based OpenClaw Dockerfile so agents that default to `python` (rather than `python3`) don't waste a tool round trip on `command not found`.

## Problem

Debian bookworm dropped the unversioned `python` command. Every OpenClaw image that ships `python3` — whether via the base image, the `OPENCLAW_DOCKER_APT_PACKAGES` apt list, or a per-solution extension Dockerfile — inherits the missing symlink.

LLM agents pretrained on a corpus where `python` is the canonical invocation reach for `python` first. Inside an OpenClaw container, that's:

1. Tool call: `python some-script.py`
2. Response: `/bin/sh: python: command not found`
3. Reasoning + retry: `python3 some-script.py`

Roughly two extra tool turns and a few hundred wasted tokens of conversation churn per attempt. Across every Python-using session, for every OpenClaw user, on every bookworm-based image, this is a steady, measurable, free-to-fix token tax. The underlying debian-conformance issue is upstream's; what this PR addresses is the gap between agent behaviour priors and image reality.

## Fix

All four Dockerfiles (`Dockerfile`, `Dockerfile.sandbox`, `Dockerfile.sandbox-browser`, `Dockerfile.sandbox-common`) get the same conditional pattern:

```dockerfile
RUN if command -v python3 >/dev/null 2>&1 && [ ! -e /usr/local/bin/python ]; then \
      ln -sf /usr/bin/python3 /usr/local/bin/python; \
    fi
```

- No-op for slim variants that don't ship python3.
- No-op if an operator has installed `python-is-python3` (the Debian-blessed alternative) — their symlink wins.
- No-op on re-build (idempotent).

## Why not `python-is-python3`?

Reasonable question. The apt package is the dpkg-blessed answer and would work for most images. I went with the conditional symlink instead because:

1. **Slim variants** may not have python3 at all. `python-is-python3` would fail an apt install there; the conditional symlink gracefully no-ops.
2. **Gateway's optional apt layer** — the main `Dockerfile`'s apt-install step is already conditional on `OPENCLAW_DOCKER_APT_PACKAGES` being set. Adding `python-is-python3` as a default apt dependency would force an apt round-trip that isn't there today.
3. **Base-image portability** — the symlink approach works if the base image is ever swapped to a non-Debian distribution (Alpine, UBI, etc.). The package is Debian-only.

If maintainers prefer the package on the gateway image specifically, happy to split that out — the sandbox Dockerfiles would still want the conditional symlink pattern for the portability reason.

## Test plan

- [x] Rebuild `openclaw:default` locally; verify `python --version` succeeds inside a running gateway container where it previously errored.
- [x] Rebuild a slim variant without python3; verify the new layer is a no-op and the image still builds.
- [x] Existing agent workflows that reach for `python` stop burning tool round trips.

## AI disclosure

AI-assisted fix, lightly tested (manual rebuild + smoke test across variants; no automated test coverage since the repo doesn't run Dockerfile-level tests). I understand what the code does.

target.title: feat(agents): add session working-context capsule (MVP for #67511)
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a small, self-contained module for persisting a per-session "working context" capsule alongside the session transcript.

This is the minimal MVP piece of #67511: the persistence primitive itself, with no runtime wiring yet. Landing the storage shape first lets `/status`, compaction, and spawn paths opt in incrementally in follow-up PRs without the shape being debated at the same time as the integration.

## Why

The JSONL transcript preserves semantic continuity — what was said and decided — but not engineering working state: active repo root, current cwd, branch, temp clones, last push target, sandbox status. After `/compact` or `/new`, this state is easy to lose even when the summary survives, which is the pain point described in #67511.

## What

New file `src/agents/session-working-context.ts`:

- `resolveWorkingContextPath(sessionFile)` → `<session>.context.json` next to the transcript, so the capsule is naturally scoped to and cleaned up with the session.
- `readWorkingContext(sessionFile)` — returns `null` on missing or malformed capsules; never throws on bad input, so older sessions just look like "no capsule".
- `writeWorkingContext(sessionFile, context)` — atomic temp + rename, `updatedAt` stamped automatically, clock is injectable for tests.
- `clearWorkingContext(sessionFile)` — for cleanup flows.
- `normalizeWorkingContext(raw)` — strips unknown/ill-typed fields so a forward-rolled capsule (e.g. from a newer build) reads back as the subset we understand.
- Envelope carries an explicit `version` field; reads accept higher versions and just ignore fields they don't know about.

Fields in the capsule today (all optional):

```ts
type SessionWorkingContext = {
  cwd?: string;
  activeRepoRoot?: string;
  branch?: string;
  tempClones?: string[];
  lastPushRemote?: string;
  lastPushBranch?: string;
  sandboxed?: boolean;
  updatedAt?: string;
  notes?: string;
};
```

## What this PR deliberately does not do

- No changes to the session manager, transcript writer, `/status`, compaction, or subagent spawn paths.
- No new config surface, no new docs pages, no migrations.
- No attempt to auto-populate the capsule from `exec`/git tools — that belongs in a follow-up once the shape lands.

The intent is to keep this PR reviewable as a pure utility module and have the behavioral integration land as separate, smaller PRs.

## Tests

`src/agents/session-working-context.test.ts` covers:

- Path resolution (with and without `.jsonl` suffix, empty-path error).
- `normalizeWorkingContext` drops unknown fields and wrong-type values, preserves `sandboxed: false`, tolerates non-object input.
- Round-trip write/read of a typical capsule.
- `readWorkingContext` returns `null` for missing / malformed / unversioned capsules.
- Forward-compatible read of a capsule with a higher `version`.
- Injected clock stamps `updatedAt`.
- Writer creates missing session directories.
- Overwrite semantics (second write replaces the first).
- `clearWorkingContext` returns `true`/`false` correctly.

## Related

- #67511 — Persist per-session working context across compact/new flows (this PR implements the MVP persistence primitive from the proposal)

## Notes for reviewers

- Kept the file inside `src/agents/` next to `session-file-repair.ts` / `session-write-lock.ts` since those are the closest existing neighbours in both shape and scope.
- Chose a sibling `.context.json` file rather than extending the JSONL header to avoid touching transcript parsing and to keep the capsule trivially rewritable without a session-write-lock round trip. Happy to move it behind the existing lock / inside the transcript if you'd prefer.
- Opening as draft because I'd like a steer on (a) whether the field set above is the right starting shape and (b) where you'd prefer the first integration point to live (`/status`, compaction pre-hook, or subagent spawn).

target.title: fix(compaction): preflight compaction uses configured compaction timeout instead of reply operation abort signal
target.body:
## Summary

Preflight (budget-triggered) compaction was hard-capped at ~60s by the reply operation's abort signal, ignoring `compaction.timeoutSeconds`. The fix composes `replyOperation.abortSignal` with `AbortSignal.timeout(resolveCompactionTimeoutMs(cfg))` so preflight compaction is bounded by the configurable timeout while still respecting explicit user abort and gateway restart signals.

- **Problem**: `runPreflightCompactionIfNeeded` passed `replyOperation.abortSignal` (~60s lifecycle) as the abort signal to `compactEmbeddedAgentSession`. Inside `compactWithSafetyTimeout`, `Promise.race([compactPromise, externalAbortPromise])` caused the compaction to fail as soon as the reply operation aborted — long before the configured `compaction.timeoutSeconds` (default 180s) could take effect. Slow compactions on large sessions could never complete.
- **Solution**: Compose `replyOperation.abortSignal` with `AbortSignal.timeout(resolveCompactionTimeoutMs(cfg))` via `AbortSignal.any()` so the timeout is config-derived (180s by default) but explicit user abort and gateway restart signals still cancel compaction.
- **What changed**: `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.ts` — 1 import + 4 lines added, 1 line removed (preflight compaction abort signal source)
- **What did NOT change**: Memory flush path's `runEmbeddedAgent` still uses `replyOperation.abortSignal` directly (unchanged). Non-preflight compaction paths (`trigger=overflow`). `compactWithSafetyTimeout` core logic. `resolveCompactionTimeoutMs` resolution.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related to #95553
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

OpenClaw runs two compaction paths with different effective timeouts:

| Path | Trigger | Effective timeout | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Overflow recovery | `trigger=overflow` | ~900s (15 min) | `resolveCompactionTimeoutMs(cfg)` |
| **Preflight / budget** | `trigger=budget` | **~60s** | `replyOperation.abortSignal` (NOT config-derived) |

The preflight path fires before every turn where `totalTokens >= contextWindow - reserveTokensFloor - softThresholdTokens`. On backends where summarizing a large context takes >60s (e.g. big window + slower self-hosted model), preflight compaction can never complete. It wastes ~60s per large turn, holds the session write lock during that time, and the turn proceeds un-compacted — only to trigger overflow recovery anyway.

The issue reporter observed: 24 of 26 incomplete compactions clustered at exactly 60–61s (the budget path), while successful compactions had median duration 278s. A 60s bound is structurally impossible for this workload.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Preflight compaction abort signal was scoped to the reply operation's ~60s lifecycle instead of the configured `compaction.timeoutSeconds`, causing compaction failures on large sessions.
- **Real environment tested**: Linux x86_64, Node 22.19+, `pnpm test`
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
# Unit test: verifies preflight compaction receives a composed signal, not raw replyOperation.abortSignal
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.test.ts --run
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.test.ts --run
 Tests  47 passed (47)
```

| Input signal source | Before fix | After fix |
|---|---|---|
| Preflight compaction `abortSignal` | `replyOperation.abortSignal` (raw, ~60s lifecycle) | `AbortSignal.any([replyOperation.abortSignal, AbortSignal.timeout(resolveCompactionTimeoutMs(cfg))])` |
| User abort propagation | Immediate via `replyOperation.abortSignal` | Immediate via `AbortSignal.any` (preserved) |
| Gateway restart abort | Immediate via `replyOperation.abortSignal` | Immediate via `AbortSignal.any` (preserved) |
| Configured timeout (e.g. 300s) | Ignored — capped by ~60s reply lifecycle | Honored — `AbortSignal.timeout(300_000)` bounds compaction |

- **Observed result after fix**:
  1. Preflight compaction abort signal is no longer the raw `replyOperation.abortSignal` — verified by reference inequality test (`expect(compactCall.abortSignal).not.toBe(replyOperation.abortSignal)`)
  2. All 46 existing tests in `agent-runner-memory.test.ts` continue to pass — no regression on existing compaction paths
  3. New test verifies `compactEmbeddedAgentSessionMock` is called with the composed signal when preflight compaction is triggered

- **What was not tested**: Live end-to-end verification with slow compaction backends (vLLM/Qwen). Unit test proves the signal composition, but real-world 60s→278s completion improvement requires a slow-backend deployment.

## Root Cause

**Provenance**: `introduced by` commit `5374c7a8a` (Peter Steinberger, 2026-05-30) which created both `compactWithSafetyTimeout` (with `Promise.race` against external abort) and `createReplyOperation` (with `AbortController`). The preflight path connected them via commit `280d1cb97` (Vincent Koc, 2026-06-09) when `abortSignal: params.replyOperation.abortSignal` was passed to `compactEmbeddedAgentSession`.

**Data flow**: `agent-runner.ts` → `runPreflightCompactionIfNeeded` (lines 981–985) → `compactEmbeddedAgentSession` → `compactWithSafetyTimeout` → `Promise.race([compactPromise, externalAbortPromise])`. The external abort from `replyOperation.abortSignal` wins at ~60s regardless of the configured `compaction.timeoutSeconds`.

**Confidence**: `clear` — source inspection confirms the signal path and unit test validates the fix.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Preflight compaction on slow backends can now complete within the configured `compaction.timeoutSeconds` (default 180s) instead of being killed at ~60s.
- No config changes required. Existing `compaction.timeoutSeconds` config now applies to the preflight path as expected.

## Security Impact

- [x] New permissions/capabilities? No
- [x] Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- [x] New/changed network calls? No
- [x] Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- [x] Data access scope changed? No

## Human Verification

- **Verified scenarios**: Preflight compaction signal composition via unit test; all 46 existing tests pass
- **Edge cases checked**: User abort signal still propagates (replyOperation.abortSignal is composed, not replaced); gateway restart signal still propagates
- **What you did NOT verify**: Live slow-backend deployment showing 60s→278s completion improvement

## Compatibility / Migration

- [x] Backward compatible? Yes — existing config keys unchanged, same behavior for fast compactions
- [ ] Config/env changes? No
- [ ] Migration needed? No

## Best-fix Verdict

- **Best fix**: Yes. The fix identifies the correct boundary — the preflight call site in `runPreflightCompactionIfNeeded` — and composes the signals so that the configured timeout takes precedence while user/restart cancellation is preserved through `replyOperation.abortSignal`. This is the minimal change that fixes both the timeout and cancellation semantics.
- **Refactor needed**: No. The `compactWithSafetyTimeout` infrastructure is correctly designed for configurable timeouts; the bug was that the preflight caller bypassed it with a short-lived signal.
- **Alternative considered**: Using the codebase's `composeAbortSignals` helper from `compaction-safety-timeout.ts` instead of `AbortSignal.any()`. Rejected because `AbortSignal.any()` is already used in 29 locations across the codebase for signal composition (e.g. `provider-transport-fetch.ts`, `model-pricing-cache.ts`, `agent-tools.abort.ts`) and is the idiomatic pattern.

## AI Assistance

- **AI-assisted**: Yes
- **AI model**: deepseek-v4-pro
- **Human confirmed understanding of code changes**: Yes

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Highest risk area**: The `AbortSignal.any()` composition adds listener overhead on `replyOperation.abortSignal`. However, this pattern is already used by `compactWithSafetyTimeout` internally (via `composeAbortSignals`), so the cost is one additional composed signal layer. No measurable impact on non-preflight paths.
- **Mitigation**: The change is scoped to the preflight compaction path only. Memory flush and overflow recovery paths are unchanged.

target.title: fix(mattermost): keep status read-only for SecretRef tokens
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `openclaw status` text mode crashed when Mattermost `botToken` used an unresolved `exec:keychain` SecretRef.
- Why it matters: Mattermost could be healthy at runtime, but read-only diagnostics failed before printing the channel table.
- What changed: Added a Mattermost read-only account inspector that reports configured-but-unavailable credential metadata without dereferencing the secret, and taught status snapshots to preserve that state.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Runtime Mattermost token resolution remains strict; this only changes read-only inspection/status behavior.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #70942
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Mattermost had strict runtime account resolution but no read-only `inspectAccount` path. Status rendering fell back to `resolveAccount`, which throws on unresolved SecretRefs.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No Mattermost regression test covered read-only status inspection with an unresolved SecretRef credential.
- Contributing context (if known): Telegram, Discord, and Slack already had read-only inspectors; Mattermost was missing the equivalent path.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/accounts.test.ts`
  - `extensions/mattermost/src/channel-actions-setup-status.contract.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: unresolved Mattermost SecretRef bot tokens are reported as configured/unavailable in read-only inspection without throwing.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: It tests the plugin resolver and the status snapshot contract directly without requiring a live Mattermost server.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`openclaw status` no longer crashes for Mattermost accounts whose bot token is configured as an unresolved SecretRef. It reports degraded credential availability instead.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
openclaw status -> Mattermost resolveAccount -> unresolved SecretRef throw -> CLI crash

After:
openclaw status -> Mattermost inspectAccount -> configured_unavailable metadata -> status table renders
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: Read-only status now detects configured SecretRefs without resolving them. It does not expose token values, and runtime resolution remains strict.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS local checkout
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Mattermost
- Relevant config (redacted): `channels.mattermost.botToken` as `exec:keychain` SecretRef plus `baseUrl`

### Steps

1. Configure Mattermost with `botToken` as an unresolved SecretRef.

target.title: fix(status): surface missing external channel plugins in summary
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`status` summary generation could still drop configured official external channels when their plugin owner was missing.

The previous patch only appended the missing-plugin repair hint inside `buildChannelSummary()`, but `getStatusSummary()` could still short-circuit before summary building when it only saw the runtime-resolved config shape. That left a gap where authored/source config still declared an official external channel, but summary mode emitted no repair hint.

This follow-up makes status summary use the same source-config view across the caller gate and summary builder, and updates announce-path detection to read authored external channel declarations from `activationSourceConfig` when the runtime config has already been normalized.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/channel-plugin-ids.test.ts src/commands/status.summary.test.ts src/infra/channel-summary.test.ts`
  - `Test Files  3 passed (3)`
  - `Tests  182 passed (182)`

## Real behavior proof

- `Behavior addressed:`
  Status summary no longer drops configured official external channels solely because the runtime config view no longer carries the authored external channel declaration; the caller gate and builder now use the same source-config view.

- `Real environment tested:`
  Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, plus packaged-environment investigation for externalized channel ownership behavior.

- `Exact steps or command run after this patch:`
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/channel-plugin-ids.test.ts src/commands/status.summary.test.ts src/infra/channel-summary.test.ts`

- `Evidence after fix:`
  - Added caller-level coverage in `src/commands/status.summary.test.ts` for projected source-config channel summary behavior.
  - Added announce-path coverage in `src/plugins/channel-plugin-ids.test.ts` for activation-source-only external channel declarations.
  - `src/infra/channel-summary.test.ts` continues to assert the rendered repair hint:
    - `Missing official external plugins:`
    - `  - Feishu: Install the official external plugin with: openclaw plugins install @openclaw/feishu, or run: openclaw doctor --fix.`

- `Observed result after fix:`
  Targeted status-summary, channel-summary, and channel announce-path regressions all pass locally after the fix.

- `What was not tested:`
  A shipped-package real CLI proof for the exact missing-owner line was investigated, but source-checkout runtime and packaged-install runtime expose different channel-owner visibility, so this PR currently relies on focused regression coverage rather than a final redacted terminal capture of the exact summary line.

target.title: fix(cron): allow webhook isolated runs to reuse sessionKey sessions
target.body:
### Motivation
- Isolated cron runs always forced a fresh session which discarded context even for `/hooks/agent` webhook calls that intentionally provide stable `sessionKey` values or `externalContentSource`; this caused unwanted session rollovers and lost history.

### Description
- Compute `forceNewSession` in `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.ts` and only force a fresh session when `sessionTarget === "isolated"` and the run is not hook/webhook-sourced, preserving hook sessions when `externalContentSource` or `hook:*` session keys are present.
- Update `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.skill-filter.test.ts` to add a regression test that verifies webhook-sourced isolated runs pass `forceNew: false` to `resolveCronSession` while keeping the existing isolated-cron behavior.
- Modified files: `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.ts`, `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.skill-filter.test.ts`.

### Testing
- Ran the targeted tests with `pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent/run.skill-filter.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/session.test.ts` and the suite passed.
- Test output: 2 test files ran, 27 tests passed (all green).

Fixes #70894

target.title: ACP: add gateway-owned node-backed runtime
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: OpenClaw did not yet have a complete **node-backed ACP runtime** where the gateway owns the durable state and a paired node acts only as the execution worker.
- Why it matters: without that split, restart/replay/reconnect behavior is too dependent on the live process, which makes lease fencing, terminal resolution, and outward delivery recovery harder to reason about and prove.
- What this PR adds:
  - a durable ACP gateway store for sessions, runs, accepted worker events, leases, terminal state, replay targets, and projector checkpoints
  - gateway ingestion of ACP worker events coming back from paired nodes
  - a real node-host ACP worker bridge and gateway/node command plumbing
  - a durable ACP reply projection/replay path so restart recovery can resume from gateway state instead of only from the live iterator
  - startup recovery for incomplete ACP node runs
  - restart/reconnect/cancel/close race hardening with focused restart/replay test coverage
  - architecture / protocol / verification docs for the new backend
- Landing-only follow-up: after rebasing onto current `main`, this PR also includes a few **test-only** isolation fixes in Feishu / Google / Google Chat extension tests that were needed to make the landing branch green. Those are not ACP runtime behavior changes.

## What changed by area

### 1) Durable ACP gateway state

New ACP store/state plumbing under `src/acp/store/**`:

- persists ACP sessions and runs
- persists accepted worker events
- tracks leases / lease epochs
- resolves canonical terminal state on the gateway
- stores replay targets and projector checkpoints separately from the runtime cursor

This is the foundation that lets the gateway stay authoritative even when execution happens on a paired node.

### 2) Gateway ↔ node ACP worker path

Main implementation areas:

- `src/gateway/**`
- `src/infra/node-commands.ts`
- `src/node-host/**`

This adds the node-backed ACP execution path:

- gateway can start/control ACP work on a paired node
- node-host can run the ACP worker role
- worker events stream back to the gateway over the existing node transport
- gateway remains the source of truth for run state and terminal resolution

### 3) Durable reply projection and replay

Main implementation areas:

- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.ts`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-replay.ts`

This PR moves ACP outward delivery onto a durable replayable path:

- reply targets are persisted per run
- projector checkpoints are advanced only after successful delivery
- restart recovery can replay the unfinished suffix from durable state
- reconnect and duplicate accepted events do not re-deliver already completed outward effects

### 4) Startup recovery / reconnect handling

Main implementation areas:

- `src/gateway/server-startup.acp-node.ts`
- `src/gateway/server-node-events.ts`
- `src/acp/runtime/acp-node.ts`

This adds the gateway-side recovery flow needed to:

- resume incomplete projection after restart
- keep reconnect handling deterministic
- prevent stale-worker wins
- preserve canonical terminal behavior across restart/replay windows

### 5) Test harnesses and regression coverage

Main test additions/expansions:

- `src/acp/store/store.restart.test.ts`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.replay.test.ts`
- `src/gateway/server-startup.acp-node.test.ts`
- `src/gateway/server-node-events.acp.test.ts`
- `src/node-host/invoke-acp.test.ts`
- `src/acp/test-harness/restart-harness.ts`

These cover the core failure windows this backend needs to survive:

- restart after accepted events but before replay checkpoint advancement
- restart after terminal persistence but before final outward convergence
- reconnect replay of already accepted events
- cancel / complete / close race handling
- restart recovery with a real memory cut instead of reusing in-process objects

### 6) Docs included in this PR

Main docs:

- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime-protocol.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime-verification.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-runtime-program.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-slice4-summary.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-slice4-final-judge.md`

These are included because this was developed as a staged architecture / verification effort and the final accepted behavior is documented there.

## Explicit scope boundaries / v1 limitations

This PR does **not**:

- turn nodes into mini-gateways
- add automatic cross-node continuation for an in-flight run
- claim durable resumed tool-message edit support after restart

Current conservative post-restart tool-update behavior remains:

- `append_only_after_restart`

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related: ACP node runtime architecture / protocol / verification docs under `docs/experiments/plans/`
- Related: landing-only extension test isolation fixes after rebase onto current `main`

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- OpenClaw now has a first-class ACP node-backed runtime path where the gateway owns durable ACP truth and paired nodes execute the worker role.
- Gateway restart/recovery can resume incomplete ACP outward projection from durable state.
- Duplicate accepted worker-event replay on reconnect is hardened so it does not duplicate outward delivery.
- Post-restart tool updates intentionally follow the documented conservative v1 rule: `append_only_after_restart`.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? Yes
- Data access scope changed? Yes

### Security / trust-boundary notes

- Paired nodes can now advertise ACP worker capability and receive ACP runtime commands over the existing gateway↔node trust boundary.
- Gateway↔node websocket traffic now carries ACP worker control / event flows in addition to existing node commands.
- Gateway now persists ACP runtime state needed for durable replay/recovery.
- The design mitigates stale-worker / duplicate-delivery risks via lease epochs, canonical terminal resolution, durable replay checkpoints, and reconnect/replay handling on the gateway.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux (landing worktree on vm1) + GitHub Actions CI
- Runtime/container: Node / pnpm; Bun build path also checked
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): ACP gateway↔node runtime path
- Relevant config (redacted): ACP runtime restart/replay harnesses and paired-node fixtures

### Verified locally

- ACP targeted verification gate:
  - `pnpm test -- src/auto-reply/reply/acp-projector.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.replay.test.ts src/acp/store/store.test.ts src/acp/store/store.restart.test.ts src/acp/store/gateway-events.test.ts src/gateway/server-node-events.acp.test.ts src/gateway/server-startup.acp-node.test.ts src/node-host/invoke-acp.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/nodes.acp.test.ts`
- TypeScript guardrail:
  - `pnpm tsgo`
- Landing-only extension test fixes:
  - `pnpm test:extensions`

### CI / build notes

- `pnpm build` still fails only with the pre-existing Bun wrapper issue:
  - `Cannot find module './cjs/index.cjs' from ''`
- That build failure was already present and is not introduced by the ACP runtime changes in this PR.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What I personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified the ACP-focused local gate listed above.
- Verified `pnpm tsgo` passes.
- Verified the rebased landing branch’s extension test-isolation fixes with local `pnpm test:extensions`.
- Verified the PR now includes the architecture / protocol / verification / final-judge docs for this backend.

What I did **not** verify:

- I did **not** get a clean `pnpm build` because of the pre-existing Bun wrapper failure noted above.
- I did **not** run a fresh manual end-to-end Discord/real-node interactive repro outside the checked-in restart/replay and node-host harnesses.

## Review Guide

If you want the shortest path through this PR, review in this order:

1. **Core architecture / intent**
   - `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime.md`
   - `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime-protocol.md`
2. **Gateway-owned durable state**
   - `src/acp/store/**`
3. **Node execution bridge**
   - `src/node-host/**`
   - `src/gateway/server-methods/nodes.ts`
   - `src/gateway/server-node-events.ts`
4. **Durable outward replay path**
   - `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts`
   - `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.ts`
   - `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-replay.ts`
5. **Recovery and proofs**
   - `src/gateway/server-startup.acp-node.ts`
   - `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.replay.test.ts`
   - `src/gateway/server-startup.acp-node.test.ts`
6. **Ignore as landing-only test fixes unless you are checking CI fallout**
   - `extensions/feishu/**`
   - `extensions/google/**`
   - `extensions/googlechat/**`

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No explicit migration required
- Migration needed? No

Notes:

- This adds a new ACP runtime/backend path; it does not force existing deployments to switch to it immediately.
- Post-restart tool updates intentionally remain append-only in v1.

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this PR or avoid selecting/using the `acp-node` runtime path until fixed.
- Files/config to restore: ACP runtime/store/gateway/node-host changes under `src/acp/**`, `src/node-host/**`, `src/gateway/**`, plus the accompanying docs if reverting wholesale.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: duplicate final delivery after restart, stale worker winning after reconnect, projector checkpoint drift, or replay targeting the wrong route.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: restart/replay correctness is subtle in any durable projection system.
  - Mitigation: this PR includes restart harnesses, startup recovery tests, reconnect/replay ordering tests, and explicit protocol/verification docs.
- Risk: the PR is large and spans multiple ACP runtime layers.
  - Mitigation: the review guide above splits it into durable store, node bridge, replay path, recovery path, and landing-only CI fixes.
- Risk: paired-node ACP execution expands the gateway↔node command surface.
  - Mitigation: the gateway remains authoritative for leases, terminal resolution, checkpoints, and replay instead of trusting node-local completion.

---

## AI Checklist

- [x] **AI-assisted** — agent-harness / Codex driven implementation, review, and landing follow-ups
- [x] **Testing degree** — focused local verification plus CI
- [x] **Understanding confirmed** — this PR adds the gateway-owned ACP node-backed runtime and includes a few landing-only extension test fixes after rebase
- [ ] Prompts/session logs — available on request

target.title: BUG: Strip strikethrough text from context files before sending to model
target.body:
Addresses #47668

Struck-through text in context files was still being forwarded to the model as-is. This PR fixes that by stripping all strikethrough content in `sanitizeContextFileContentForPrompt()` before the context is embedded in the system prompt. It handles both markdown (`~~text~~`) and HTML variants (`<s>`, `<del>`, `<strike>`), including multiline blocks. The content itself is removed entirely, not just the markers.

Changes are in `src/agents/system-prompt.ts` and `src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts`. Tests added for both inline and multiline cases.

target.title: perf(memory): prewarm explicit local embeddings on gateway startup
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: builtin `memory_search` with explicit `memorySearch.provider = "local"` still pays model/context initialization on the first post-startup search because gateway startup only warmed QMD.
- Why it matters: the first memory-backed reply after a gateway restart absorbs the local embedding cold start instead of the post-attach startup path.
- What changed: extend `startGatewayMemoryBackend` to prewarm builtin local managers by calling `probeEmbeddingAvailability()` after manager creation, and add gateway startup tests for explicit-local, skip, and failure logging paths.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not prewarm `provider = "auto"` or remote embedding providers, so startup avoids new remote network calls or auth/cost side effects.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #70460
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: builtin memory with explicit `memorySearch.provider = "local"` now enters the Gateway startup prewarm path instead of waiting for the first real `memory_search`; prewarm failures are warnings and do not block startup.
- Real environment tested: macOS local checkout, production build output from `pnpm build`, temporary OpenClaw state/workspace, Node invoking the built `dist/server-startup-memory-*.js` module.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran the built startup module with a temporary config using `memory.backend=builtin`, `agents.defaults.memorySearch.provider=local`, vector disabled, and a temporary `memory/MEMORY.md` file.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

```text
proof module: server-startup-memory-DNxA_AEE.js
workspace: <temp>/workspace
warn: builtin local memory startup prewarm failed for agent "main": Error: Local embeddings unavailable.
Reason: optional dependency node-llama-cpp is missing (or failed to install).
Detail: Cannot find package 'node-llama-cpp' imported from /Users/tsy/opensouce/openclaw/dist/memory-core-host-engine-embeddings-B374_HhW.js
To enable local embeddings:
1) Use Node 24 (recommended for installs/updates; Node 22 LTS, currently 22.16+, remains supported)
2) Install node-llama-cpp next to the OpenClaw package or source checkout
3) If you use pnpm: pnpm approve-builds (select node-llama-cpp), then pnpm rebuild node-llama-cpp
Or set agents.defaults.memorySearch.provider = "github-copilot" (remote).
Or set agents.defaults.memorySearch.provider = "openai" (remote).
Or set agents.defaults.memorySearch.provider = "gemini" (remote).
Or set agents.defaults.memorySearch.provider = "voyage" (remote).
Or set agents.defaults.memorySearch.provider = "mistral" (remote).
Or set agents.defaults.memorySearch.provider = "deepinfra" (remote).
Or set agents.defaults.memorySearch.provider = "bedrock" (remote).
result: startup hook completed with 1 log event(s)
```

- Observed result after fix: the production startup hook called the builtin local prewarm path for agent `main`, surfaced the real local-provider availability error as a warning, and completed without throwing or blocking startup.
- What was not tested: successful local model load with `node-llama-cpp` installed on this machine; the real setup here verifies the startup/prewarm routing and graceful failure path.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): before this patch, builtin backends were skipped by `startGatewayMemoryBackend` unless the resolved backend was QMD, so this same builtin/local startup path did not call the memory manager prewarm probe.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: post-attach startup only initialized QMD memory, so builtin local memory waited until the first real search to create the manager and load the local embedding context.
- Missing detection / guardrail: gateway startup coverage did not assert builtin explicit-local prewarm behavior or failure logging.
- Contributing context (if known): `purpose: "status"` managers bypass the builtin cache, so a status-only probe would not have helped the first real search.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/server-startup-memory.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: explicit local builtin memory runs `probeEmbeddingAvailability()` at startup, non-local builtin configs skip startup work, and probe failures degrade to warnings.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it exercises the gateway startup decision point without requiring real local model assets.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing QMD startup tests in the same file.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Gateways using builtin memory with `agents.*.memorySearch.provider = "local"` now shift local embedding cold-start work into post-attach startup, so the first real `memory_search` request avoids paying that initialization cost.
- No behavior change for QMD, `provider = "auto"`, or remote memory embedding providers.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
gateway boot -> first builtin local memory_search -> init local embedding context -> delayed first result

After:
gateway boot -> post-attach startup prewarm -> first builtin local memory_search -> reuse warmed context
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node.js via `pnpm`
- Model/provider: builtin memory with explicit local embedding provider
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): `memory.backend=builtin`, `agents.defaults.memorySearch.provider=local`

### Steps

1. Start the gateway with builtin memory and an explicit local memory provider.
2. Let post-attach startup run.
3. Trigger the first `memory_search` request after startup.

### Expected

- The first real search reuses a manager whose local embedding provider has already been prewarmed.
- Non-local builtin configs still skip startup warmup.
- Failed prewarm attempts only log warnings.

### Actual

- Before this change, only QMD was warmed at startup, so builtin local memory paid the cold-start cost on the first real search.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: explicit local memory configs now do extra startup work and could log a warning earlier in the boot cycle.
- Mitigation: the prewarm runs only for `memory.backend=builtin` plus explicit `provider=local`, after post-attach startup, and failures degrade to warnings without blocking startup.
